---
title: "Event handling in PEPRMT"
author:
  - "Chris Black"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
toc: true
number-sections: true
fig-width: 10
fig-height: 6
fig-dpi: 300
---

# Introduction

Welcome to the third demo workbook for `PEcAn.PEPRMT`!

## Purpose

Through the previous demo workbooks, we have shown how to execute a
minimal PEcAn run (`01_run_pecan_peprmt.qmd`), as well as how to set the
parameters and input data for that run (`02_input_demo.qmd`). Here, we
build on the outputs from `02_input_demo.qmd` by showing how to add
management events to a PEPRMT run in PEcAn.

For demonstration, the events we illustrate here include a one-time step
change in inundation depth and a temporary fractional change in
salinity. The same approach could be extended to other event types that
alter timeseries inputs.

Future development will add support for events that alter model
parameters, e.g. changes in site vegetation type. This will require new
capabilities for "restarting" the model, which exist for other PEcAn
models but are not yet implemented for PEPRMT.

## Prerequisites

We assume here that you already have PEcAn and PEPRMT installed. See
`01_run_pecan_peprmt.qmd` for installation instructions if needed.

We also assume you have already run `02_input_demo.qmd`. This demo uses
these files created by in the second demp:

- `settings_inputdemo.xml`
- `data/met/`
- `data/PEPRMT_specific_inputs/`
- `data/pfts/default_marginal/`

## Setup

```{r}
#| label: setup
here::i_am("models/peprmt/demo_run/event_demo.qmd")

# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(PEcAn.all)
library(PEcAn.PEPRMT)
PEcAn.all::pecan_version()

# Set PEcAn loggers
logger.setUseConsole(console = TRUE, stderr = FALSE)
logger.setLevel("INFO")
```

### Define outdirs

PEcAn accepts existing output directories and will attempt to overwrite
them, but this often does not work cleanly. We advise always starting
fresh.

This check adds a step when trying to rerun the notebook quickly, but
complaining here is better than clobbering outputs you were counting on.

```{r}
#| label: outdir-check
outdirs <- c(noevt = "event_demo_out_noevt", evt = "event_demo_out_evt")
have_outdirs <- dir.exists(outdirs)
if (any(have_outdirs)) {
  logger.severe(
    "Please move or delete outdir", sQuote(outdirs[have_outdirs]), "first"
  )
}
```

## Settings

For brevity let's modify the settings used for the input demo rather
than start from scratch. We'll use only the first two sites (US_EDN and
US_SRR) and add an event file.

To add an input to a PEcAn run, first declare it in the sampling space
and then provide (an ensemble of) paths to the input files in each
site's input block. Note that these files don't exist yet -- we'll
create them next. This step only constructs paths and does not check
their contents.

```{r}
#| label: settings
settings <- read.settings("settings_inputdemo.xml")
settings$pfts$pft$posterior.files <-
  file.path("data", "pfts", "default_marginal", "post.distns.RData")

settings_noevt <- settings[1:2] |>
  setOutDir(outdirs[["noevt"]])
settings_noevt <- PEcAn.settings::prepare.settings(settings_noevt)

settings_evt <- settings[1:2] |>
  setOutDir(outdirs[["evt"]]) |>
  setEnsemblePaths(
    n_reps = as.numeric(settings$ensemble$size),
    input_type = "event_json",
    path = file.path("data", "events"),
    # For prototype at least, making one JSON file per ensemble member,
    # with each one containing events from all sites => no site_id in path
    path_template = "{path}/events_{n}.json"
  )

settings_evt$ensemble$samplingspace$event_json <- list(method="sampling")
settings_evt <- PEcAn.settings::prepare.settings(settings_evt)

write.settings(
  settings_noevt,
  outputfile = "settings_eventdemo_noevt.xml",
  outputdir = getwd()
)
write.settings(
  settings_evt,
  outputfile = "settings_eventdemo_evt.xml",
  outputdir = getwd()
)
```

## Event files

For demo purposes let's imagine all sites experience a one-time
permanent change in inundation depth of approximately 30 cm and a
temporary step change in salinity of approximately 75 percent. These are
chosen more for simplicity than for biological realism; suggestions for
other simple event examples are very welcome.

PEcAn will treat these as "management events", and stores them as nested
lists in JSON format. Each site has a site id, a list of events, and
optionally other site-level metadata. Each event has a date, an event
type, and optionally other parameters needed to specify the particulars
of that event type. Models are allowed to ignore event types they do not
recognize and to disregard event parameters not used in their
representation of an event they do recognize.

```{r}
#| label: write-event-json

dir.create(file.path("data", "events"))
for (i in seq_len(settings$ensemble$size))  {
  dsal <- rnorm(n = 1, mean = 75, sd = 5) |> round(2)
  delev <- rnorm(n = 1, mean = 30, sd = 20) |> round(2)
  evts <- list(
    list(
      pecan_events_version = "0.1.2",
      site_id = "US_EDN",
      events = list(
        list(
          event_type = "salinity",
          date = "2019-01-01",
          pct_relative_salinity_change = -dsal,
          days_duration = 180
        ),
        list(
          event_type = "elevation",
          date = "2020-01-01",
          cm_elevation_rise = delev
        )
      )
    ),
    list(
      pecan_events_version = "0.1.2",
      site_id = "US_SRR",
      events = list(
        list(
          event_type = "salinity",
          date = "2015-01-01",
          pct_relative_salinity_change = dsal,
          days_duration = 730
        ),
        list(
          event_type = "elevation",
          date = "2016-01-01",
          cm_elevation_rise = delev
        )
      )
    )
  )

  jsonlite::write_json(
    x = evts,
    path = file.path("data", "events", paste0("events_", i, ".json")),
    pretty = TRUE,
    auto_unbox = TRUE
  )
}


```

## Set up run

### Generate sample design

To get clean comparisons between treatments, we'll pair simulations with
and without events by using the same ensemble of parameter and input
values for both runs.

```{r}
#| label: design

evt_design <- generate_joint_ensemble_design(
  settings_evt[[1]],
  settings_evt$ensemble$size
)$X

noevt_design <- evt_design |>
  select(-event_json)

settings_evt$ensemble$id <- rlang::hash(evt_design)
settings_noevt$ensemble$id <- rlang::hash(noevt_design)
```

### Write config files

```{r}
#| label: write-config
settings_evt <- runModule.run.write.configs(
  settings_evt,
  input_design = evt_design
)

file.copy(
  file.path(settings_evt$outdir, "samples.Rdata"),
  file.path(settings_noevt$outdir, "samples.Rdata")
)
settings_noevt <- runModule.run.write.configs(
  settings_noevt,
  input_design = noevt_design
)

write.csv(evt_design, file.path(settings_evt$outdir, "input_design.csv"))
write.csv(noevt_design, file.path(settings_noevt$outdir, "input_design.csv"))
```

### Run PEPRMT

```{r}
#| label: run
runModule_start_model_runs(settings_noevt)
runModule_start_model_runs(settings_evt)
```

## Plot results

Most non-carbon outputs are not yet written into the PEcAn standard
outputs, so let's read read the raw PEPRMT output (`out.csv`) to verify
the environment changes. See the input demo for an example of reading
from PEcAn netcdf output.

```{r}
#| label: read
runid <- read.csv(file.path(settings_noevt$outdir, "runs_manifest.csv"))$run_id
outdirs <- file.path(settings_noevt$outdir, "out", runid)

model_output_noevt <- purrr::map2_dfr(
  .x = runid |> setNames(nm = _),
  .y = outdirs,
  .f = \(id, dir) read.csv(file.path(dir, "out.csv")),
  .id = "run_id"
) |>
  mutate(
    date = parse_date_time(paste(Year, DOY_disc), "yj"),
    site_id = sub("ENS-\\d+-", "", run_id),
    ens_num = sub("ENS-(\\d+).*", "\\1", run_id)
  )

runid <- read.csv(file.path(settings_evt$outdir, "runs_manifest.csv"))$run_id
outdirs <- file.path(settings_evt$outdir, "out", runid)

model_output_evt <- purrr::map2_dfr(
  .x = runid |> setNames(nm = _),
  .y = outdirs,
  .f = \(id, dir) read.csv(file.path(dir, "out.csv")),
  .id = "run_id"
) |>
  mutate(
    date = parse_date_time(paste(Year, DOY_disc), "yj"),
    site_id = sub("ENS-\\d+-", "", run_id),
    ens_num = sub("ENS-(\\d+).*", "\\1", run_id)
  )

model_output <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  no_event = model_output_noevt,
  event = model_output_evt,
  .id = "has_event"
) |>
  mutate(
    cum_CH4 = cumsum(CH4_mod),
    cum_Reco = cumsum(Reco_mod),
    cum_GPP = cumsum(GPP_mod),
    .by = c(ens_num, site_id, has_event)
  )

```

Salinity and water table depth did change:

```{r}
#| label: plot-treatments
# base_plot <- ggplot(model_output) +
#   aes(x = date, color = has_event) +
#   facet_grid(ens_num ~ site_id, scales = "free_x") +
#   geom_line() +
#   theme_bw()
base_plot <- ggplot(model_output) +
  aes(x = date, color = has_event, group = paste(has_event, ens_num)) +
  facet_wrap(~ site_id, scales = "free") +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  theme_bw()

base_plot + aes(y = Salinity_daily_ave_ppt)
base_plot + aes(y = WTD_cm)
```

...And as expected, salinity reduction at the normally-salty EDN site
produces a visible increase in CH4 production, while salinity increase
at the mostly-fresh SRR site produces a net inhibition of CH4. The
change in water depth does not visibly affect CH4 in these simulations.

```{r}
#| label: plot-ch4
base_plot + aes(y = log(1 + CH4_mod))
# base_plot + aes(y = log(GPP_mod) * sign(GPP_mod))
# base_plot + aes(y = log(1 + Reco_mod))

base_plot + aes(y = cum_CH4) + scale_y_log10()
# base_plot + aes(y = cum_GPP)
# base_plot + aes(y = cum_Reco)
```
